item.title: Bare Metal Programming on Pinephone -- part 3
item.description: We can now use U-Boot to load our application image. Application Image In part 1, we built a dead-simple Arm64 application. But we probably cannot see any difference on Pinephone if we load a program that only does an addition. Yet, without a proper OS, what we can do is limited. I follow the idea to write a test program to toggle the LED. Let’s look at PinePhone’s mainboard schemetic, which you can find on PinePhone’s wiki.

item.title: Bare Metal Programming on Pinephone -- part 2
item.description: In part one, we learned how to build a bare-metal program for Arm64. Now, let’s try to run the program on Pinephone. Boot loader To run our program, we will need a boot loader to load our executable on to the memory. From linux-sunxi wiki, A Allwinner processor is expecting U-Boot on storage devices. U-Boot U-Boot is a boot loader for many embedded boards. I follow the guide here to build it.

item.title: Bare-Metal Programming on Pinephone -- part 1
item.description: Recently, I got a Pinephone and planned to write a hobby mobile OS for fun. The first step is to see if I can write a bare-metal program onto Pinephone. It turned out to be not very difficult and I will document the steps here for anyone interested. Preparation We need several things: A Pinephone, of course. It will be extremely helpful if it has a bootable Linux OS. An USB to serial audio jack connector.
